walkthrough.title: Place the Reef and Ocean Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Oceans set up. Find the Reef board and the Ocean board and place them in the center of the play area where everyone can reach. The Ocean board has three zones stacked vertically, each with a shelf for holding population tokens and Scenario cards. Stack the 24 species boards nearby where all players can grab them during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Cambrian Explosion Card
walkthrough.text: Find the Cambrian Explosion card. It has two sides: one labeled 'Reef Variant' and one for the standard game. If anyone at the table has never played Oceans, we recommend the Reef Variant — place it with the Reef Variant side face-up in the 1st Ocean zone. For experienced players, place it face-down in the 1st Ocean zone so the standard side shows.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Scenario Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the 25 Scenario cards. Shuffle them and place 1 random Scenario card on the shelf of the 1st Ocean zone and 1 random Scenario card on the shelf of the 2nd Ocean zone. Return the remaining Scenario cards to the box. If you are playing the Reef Variant, skip the random draw and instead use the Abundance and Fertile Scenario cards specifically.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal the Surface Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the 120 Surface cards. These are the cards with 12 different traits, 10 copies of each. Give them a good shuffle and deal 6 cards face-down to each player. Then place the remaining Surface deck face-down next to the Reef. These represent the known world of traits — things already discovered by science.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Deep Deck and Gene Pool
walkthrough.text: Find the 89 Deep cards. Every card in the Deep is unique and very powerful. Shuffle the Deep deck and place two random cards face-up next to it — these form the Gene Pool, which gives players a choice of Deep cards to draft later. If you are playing the Reef Variant, skip this step entirely and leave the Deep deck in the box.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside the Reserve Population
walkthrough.text: Find the 200 population tokens. Take 60 of them and place them in the 'reserve' zip-lock bag. Set this bag aside — you will not use these tokens until the very last round of the game, when they get added to the 3rd Ocean zone.

walkthrough.title: Create the Population Supply
walkthrough.text: Now create your population supply based on your player count. The formula is 60 population plus 20 per player. For 2 players, use 100 tokens. For 3 players, use 120 tokens. For 4 players, use 140 tokens. Return any unused tokens to the box — they are not used this game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Population to the Board
walkthrough.text: Take your supply of population tokens and roughly divide them into 4 piles. Place one pile on the Reef and one pile in each of the 3 Ocean zones. These piles do not need to be exact — just spread them out roughly evenly across the four locations.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Starting Migration
walkthrough.text: Reveal the top card from the Surface deck and place it face-up to start the discard pile. Look at the Migrate number on the bottom left of that card. Move that number of population from the Reef into the 3rd Ocean zone, and also move that same number of population from the 1st Ocean zone into the 3rd Ocean zone. This shifts some early population toward the deep ocean.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Screens and Bonus Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each player one of the 4 Player Screens. These keep your score pile hidden during the game. Now determine the start player — the rulebook says it is the person who can hold their breath the longest. Give each player the correct Bonus Token based on turn order. In a 2-player game: 1st player gets 4 points, 2nd gets 0. In a 3-player game: 6, 4, 0 points. In a 4-player game: 7, 6, 4, 0 points. Place your Bonus Token behind your Player Screen.

bullets: Place Reef and Ocean board centrally
Ocean has 3 zones with shelves for population and Scenario cards
Stack 24 species boards nearby

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Goal
walkthrough.text: The goal of Oceans is to thrive in an ever-changing ecosystem where food is scarce and predators lurk. You will create species, evolve them with trait cards, and grow their populations by foraging from the Reef, attacking other species, or passively gaining from the Ocean. At the end of the game, you score 1 point for each population token you accumulated in your score pile behind your Player Screen, plus 1 point for each population on your surviving species, plus the points on your Bonus Token. Highest score wins.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Players take turns in clockwise order, starting with the start player. Each turn has exactly 4 phases, always in this order: Phase 1 is Playing Cards — you play 1 card (or 2 after the Cambrian Explosion). Phase 2 is Feeding — you feed 1 of your species. Phase 3 is Aging — all of your species age, scoring population. Phase 4 is Drawing Cards — you discard unwanted cards and draw back up to 6.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Playing Cards — Evolve
walkthrough.text: Before the Cambrian Explosion, you play 1 Surface card per turn. Most cards will be played as traits to evolve your species. To play a trait on a new species, take a free species board from the supply and place it so the fish face away from you. A new species can go to the left, right, or between any two of your existing species. Place the trait card along the dark-blue border on the left side of the species board. To play a trait on an existing species, just tuck it along the same border. A species can have multiple copies of the same trait (effects are cumulative), but the maximum number of traits is 3 unless a card explicitly allows more.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Playing Cards — Migrate
walkthrough.text: Instead of evolving, you can use your card to migrate population. Look at the Migrate number on the bottom left of the card you play. Choose a board location — either the Reef or one of the three Ocean zones. Discard the card and move that number of population tokens to your chosen location from one other board location. If the Migrate number is greater than the available population, only move what is available. You can also remove traits from your species anytime during this phase. Discarded Surface cards go face-up to the discard pile; removed Deep cards are removed from the game entirely.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Feeding — Forage
walkthrough.text: Choose 1 of your species to feed. It can either forage or attack. To forage, add up all the green forage icons on the feeding species' traits to get its forage value. Take that many population tokens from the Reef and place them on the species board. A species without any green icons automatically gets a forage value of 1. Important: a species may never forage if it has an attack-only restriction icon, regardless of other forage icons. A species cannot forage or attack unless it would take at least 1 population, and it cannot take more population than it has open spaces on its species board.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Feeding — Attack
walkthrough.text: To attack, choose any other species as a target — including your own species. Add up the red attack icons on the feeding species' traits to get its attack value. Take that many population tokens from the target's species board and place them on the attacker's species board. A species without any red icons automatically gets an attack value of 1. A species may never attack if it has a forage-only restriction icon. Remember: you must take the full attack value if possible — you cannot choose to take fewer to avoid overpopulation.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Rules and Overpopulation
walkthrough.text: Each species board has 10 spaces for population tokens, but the last space has a fishbones icon. You may never place a population token on the fishbones space unless it is the only available space. After each feeding, resolve all triggered effects, then check for overpopulation. A species overpopulates if it has a token on the fishbones space. When this happens, the species loses population until only 5 remain on the board. The owner places the lost population on the Reef or in any one Ocean zone. If a species overpopulates during its own feeding, it cannot feed again this turn, even if it has a trait like Tentacled that normally allows extra feeding.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Traits and Shell Icons
walkthrough.text: Traits with black textboxes are defensive traits that may prevent an attack. Some defensive traits have a shell icon with a number inside. Each shell icon reduces the number of population taken from an attack by the number shown. Shell icons do not reduce the attacker's attack value — they just reduce how many population tokens the attacker actually takes. For example, if a species attacks with a value of 8 and the target has shell icons totaling 8, the attacker takes 0 population from that target.

walkthrough.title: Adjacency and Gains
walkthrough.text: Every species in Oceans lives in the same environment. The left-most species of each player is adjacent to the right-most species of the next player clockwise. Many traits trigger based on the feeding of an adjacent species — the arrow icon at the bottom of a trait refers to the species immediately next to it on that side. Feeding may also trigger gains icons on other species, including species belonging to other players. When a species gains population, it takes the number shown in the blue gains icon from the 1st Ocean zone. If the 1st zone is empty, take from the 2nd, then the 3rd. If the 3rd is also empty, place the 60 reserve population into the 3rd Ocean zone so every triggered trait takes its proper amount.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Aging
walkthrough.text: After feeding, all of your species age. Before the Cambrian Explosion, remove 1 population from each of your species and place it behind your Player Screen in your score pile. After the Cambrian Explosion starts, you score 2 population from each species instead of 1. Score all remaining population if a species cannot age the full amount. A species goes extinct if it cannot age the full amount — discard all its traits and return the species board to the communal supply. Important: only 1 species feeds per turn, but all of your species age every turn. If a species reaches 0 population on another player's turn, it does not go extinct — you get a chance to feed it before your next aging phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: First, you may draw 1 Deep card using one of two methods. You can take the top face-up card from a Gene Pool pile (if a pile is emptied, flip the top card of the Deep deck to form a new pile). Or you can draw 3 cards from the Deep deck, keep 1, and place the other 2 face-up on each Gene Pool pile. Second, you may discard any number of Surface cards from your hand. You may not discard Deep cards. Third, draw from the Surface deck until you have 6 total cards in hand (Surface and Deep combined). If the Surface deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new deck. Your turn is now over, and play passes to the left.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Cards
walkthrough.text: There are 2 types of Scenario cards. Blue Scenario cards have ongoing effects and are active whenever there is no population in their Ocean zone. Purple Scenario cards have events that trigger each time their Ocean zone is emptied — this can happen multiple times if population is migrated back onto the zone. Scenario cards activate when the last population is removed from their Ocean zone. Always resolve all triggered effects (like gains and overpopulation) before activating the Scenario card. Scenario card icons help tailor the game: teeth icons mean more aggressive gameplay, diamond icons are more complex, and lightning icons are events.

walkthrough.title: The Cambrian Explosion
walkthrough.text: The Cambrian Explosion starts when the 1st Ocean zone becomes completely empty of population. Remove the Cambrian Explosion card as a reminder that it is permanent. If it begins during your playing cards phase, you may immediately play a second card. If it begins during a later phase, you do not get to go back and play a second card that turn. During the Cambrian Explosion: you play 2 cards per turn instead of 1 (Surface and/or Deep), you score 2 population per species during aging instead of 1, and you may now play Deep cards. Deep cards have an extra cost to evolve — you must pay population equal to the card's Migrate number from your score pile to the Reef or any one Ocean zone.

walkthrough.title: The Deep Cards
walkthrough.text: Every Deep card is unique, powerful, and has a cost if used to evolve a species. To evolve with a Deep card: first pay the cost by moving population equal to its Migrate number from your score pile to the Reef or any one Ocean zone. Then place the Deep trait on a new or existing species. To use a Deep card for migration instead, you do not pay any cost — just discard the card from the game after resolving the migration. The Deep deck can be customized over time. After each game, you can remove Deep cards you did not enjoy and add extra copies of ones you loved.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The last round triggers when every Ocean zone is completely empty of population. Place the 60 reserve population tokens into the 3rd Ocean zone and continue playing normally. The game ends when the final player in turn order completes their turn, even if population remains in the Reef. Players score: 1 point for each population in their score pile, 1 point for each population on their surviving species, and the points listed on their Bonus Token. Highest score wins. In a tie, the tied player with the most trait cards on their species wins. If still tied, the rulebook suggests the first person to get a job on a fishing boat in Alaska wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Game
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round of Oceans with three players — the recommended player count. Player 1 goes first, Player 2 second, and Player 3 third. Each player will take different actions so you can see how the core mechanics work together. We are playing the Reef Variant for simplicity — no Deep cards, using the Abundance and Fertile Scenario cards. Follow along even if your table has a different number of players.

walkthrough.title: Starting State
walkthrough.text: The board is set up with 120 population tokens (3-player game) divided roughly among the Reef and 3 Ocean zones — about 30 tokens in each location. Each player has 6 Surface cards in hand and a Player Screen. The Cambrian Explosion card sits in the 1st Ocean zone with the Reef Variant side face-up. The Abundance Scenario is on the 1st Ocean zone shelf, and Fertile is on the 2nd. Player 1 has a 6-point Bonus Token, Player 2 has 4 points, and Player 3 has 0.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Create a New Species
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks at their hand and finds a trait with a green forage icon. Perfect for building a foraging species. For Phase 1, Player 1 takes a species board from the supply and places a trait with 1 green forage icon on it. The species is now in play with no population — it starts empty. For Phase 2, Player 1 chooses to feed this new species by foraging. It has 1 forage icon, so it takes 1 population token from the Reef and places it on the species board. For Phase 3, aging: Player 1 removes 1 population from the species and places it behind their Player Screen. The species now has 0 population, but it does not go extinct because it aged the full amount (1). For Phase 4, Player 1 draws back up to 6 cards.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Opening
walkthrough.text: Player 1 did three things right in one turn. They created a species, fed it, and scored a population token. Even though the species ended the turn with 0 population, that is fine — it aged the full amount so it survives. On Player 1's next turn, they can feed it again to rebuild population. The key insight: every population token that goes behind your Player Screen is 1 point. Start scoring from turn 1.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Create a Bigger Forager
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays a trait with 2 green forage icons on a new species. During feeding, this species forages for 2 population from the Reef — already twice as effective as Player 1's species. During aging, Player 2 scores 1 population behind their Screen. The species retains 1 population token on the board heading into the next round. Player 2 draws back up to 6 cards. Notice how having more forage icons means the species can accumulate population faster and still have some left after aging.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: The First Mistake — Ignoring Population
walkthrough.text: Player 3 looks at their hand and sees three different trait cards they want to play. They decide to create a new species with a trait that has no forage and no attack icons. During feeding, this species has a default forage value of 1, so it takes 1 population from the Reef. During aging, it must score 1 population — and now it has 0 population. That is fine, the species survives. But here is the mistake: Player 3 is already thinking about evolving this species with more traits next turn instead of focusing on building up population. Without population, traits are useless.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Population Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Here is the lesson from Player 3's turn. In Oceans, population tokens are your only source of points. You can have the most amazing combination of traits in the game, but if your species cannot accumulate population, those traits score you nothing. Before stacking more traits, make sure your species can actually feed effectively. A species with a single forage-2 trait is often better than a species loaded with three fancy traits that cannot sustain itself through aging.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn: Add a Second Trait
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays a second trait on their existing species — this one has 1 more green forage icon. Now the species has a total forage value of 2 (the icons are cumulative). During feeding, it takes 2 population from the Reef. During aging, it scores 1 population. It retains 1 population on the board. Player 1 is building a solid foraging engine. Two traits on one species, cumulative forage value, and a population surplus after aging.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Turn: Create a Second Species
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays a trait with a red attack icon on a new species. Now Player 2 has two species: a forager and an attacker. For feeding, Player 2 chooses to feed the forager (not the attacker), pulling 2 population from the Reef. During aging, both species lose 1 population each. The forager goes from 3 to 2 population. The attacker has no population yet, so it ages 0 — but since it could not age the full amount of 1, it does not go extinct. Wait, actually it does! A species goes extinct if it cannot age the full amount. The attacker had 0 population and needed to score 1. Player 2 loses the attacker and its trait card.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Stacking Traits on an Empty Species
walkthrough.text: Player 3 ignores the lesson and plays a second trait on their species — now it has 2 traits but still has 0 population going into feeding. Player 3 feeds for the default 1 forage (neither trait has forage icons). During aging, the species must score 1 population but only has 1. It scores 1 and drops to 0. Player 3 spent two cards evolving a species that cannot sustain itself. Those two trait cards are wasted if the species goes extinct, which is likely because it always ends the turn at 0 population — one bad break and it is gone.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Feed Before You Evolve
walkthrough.text: Player 3 kept adding traits instead of focusing on traits that help the species eat. In Oceans, the most important trait on any species is the one that puts food on the board. Prioritize forage icons or attack icons first. Only add utility or defensive traits once your species can reliably maintain population through aging. Think of it this way: a species needs to eat more than it ages each turn, or it is just treading water.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Third Turn: Migrate for Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has a card with a high Migrate number — say, 4. Instead of evolving, they play this card to migrate 4 population from the 1st Ocean zone to the Reef. Why? Because their forager eats from the Reef, and moving population there means more food available. This is a subtle but powerful move. Migration lets you stock the locations where your species feeds. It also moves population away from locations where your opponents' species might benefit.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Third Turn: Learning from the Extinction
walkthrough.text: Player 2 learned the hard way that a species needs population to survive. This turn, they play a trait with a red attack icon on a new species and immediately choose to feed it. The attacker targets Player 1's forager and takes 1 population. Now the attacker has 1 population. During aging, it scores that 1 and drops to 0 — surviving because it aged the full amount. Player 2 also ages the forager, which scores 1 from its remaining population. This time, Player 2 fed the new species on the same turn they created it.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Redemption: Starting Fresh
walkthrough.text: Player 3 finally takes the lesson to heart. They play a trait with 2 green forage icons on a brand new species. During feeding, this new species forages 2 from the Reef. During aging, Player 3 scores 1 population from each species. The old 2-trait species drops to 0, barely surviving. The new forager retains 1 population. Player 3 is back in the game with a species that can actually sustain itself. The old species might need to be abandoned, but the new one has a future.

walkthrough.title: Watching the Reef Drain
walkthrough.text: Notice what is happening to the board. Each turn, species forage from the Reef, and those tokens go onto species boards. During aging, they move behind Player Screens. Population is flowing from the board into players' score piles. The Reef will run low. When the 1st Ocean zone empties, the Cambrian Explosion begins and the game accelerates — you play 2 cards and age 2 population per species. Keep an eye on the Ocean zones because when all 3 empty, the last round triggers.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the core flow of Oceans: create species, evolve them with traits, feed one species per turn, age all your species for points, and draw cards. The game builds as population flows around the board — from the Reef and Ocean zones onto species, through aging into your score pile. As you play more turns, you will add more traits, create more species, and eventually trigger the Cambrian Explosion, which doubles the pace of evolution and scoring.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through a scripted opening of Oceans and watched Player 3 learn from two common mistakes: ignoring population growth and stacking traits on species that cannot sustain themselves. The most important takeaway is that population is everything in this game. Traits are tools to help your species eat — they are not the goal themselves. Focus on feeding first, scoring through aging, and evolving your species around population growth.

walkthrough.title: Before You Dive In
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for beginner strategies around the Reef, the Cambrian Explosion timing, and how to evaluate traits.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick glossary of every Surface trait, Scenario type, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun exploring the deep!

All 3 Ocean zones empty = final round triggers

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Core loop: create species, evolve, feed, age, draw
Game accelerates when Cambrian Explosion starts (2 cards, 2 aging)
Build toward sustainable species that generate consistent points

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced all 4 phases of a turn
Watched Player 3 learn from two common mistakes
Key takeaway: population is everything — traits serve feeding

summary.title: Before You Dive In
bullets: Check out General Tips for beginner strategies
Reference the Appendix for trait and term glossary
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via the Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, building too many species at once — each species must age every turn, so more species means more population drain. Second, ignoring the Reef — if the Reef runs dry, foragers starve. Third, stacking traits on species that cannot feed themselves. Fourth, hoarding Deep cards without the score pile to pay for them. Fifth, forgetting that all species age every turn, not just the one you fed. These five mistakes account for most new-player losses.

walkthrough.title: How Many Species Should You Run?
walkthrough.text: This is the most important strategic question in Oceans. Every species must age each turn, costing you 1 population each (2 after the Cambrian Explosion). Running four species means 4 population drained per turn just to keep them alive. If you cannot feed enough to cover that drain, your species start going extinct and you lose the traits you invested. Start with one or two well-fed species. Only create a third when you are confident your existing species can sustain themselves through aging without your help — for example, species with high forage values that accumulate faster than they drain.

walkthrough.title: The Reef Is Your Lifeline
walkthrough.text: The Reef is the primary food source for foraging species, and it does not replenish on its own. Once it runs out, it stays empty unless a Scenario card, Deep card, or overpopulation puts population back into it. This means the Reef is a shared, limited resource. If you build a heavy foraging strategy, keep an eye on the Reef's population level. Use migration to move population from Ocean zones into the Reef when it runs low. If the Reef empties and you have no attack species, you are stuck.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Population Flow
walkthrough.text: Population flows in a predictable cycle. It starts on the board — in the Reef and Ocean zones. Species take it through foraging, attacking, or gains. It sits on species boards temporarily. During aging, it moves to score piles. This is one-way: once population is in your score pile, it stays there (unless you pay it to play Deep cards). The total supply is finite. In a 3-player game, that is 120 tokens in play plus 60 in reserve. Understanding this flow helps you predict when the Cambrian Explosion will hit and when the game will end.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Cambrian Explosion
walkthrough.text: The Cambrian Explosion triggers when the 1st Ocean zone empties. After that, everything doubles: you play 2 cards per turn and age 2 population per species. This is a huge acceleration. If you have well-fed species with population reserves, the Cambrian Explosion is great — you score twice as fast. If your species are barely surviving, the increased aging will kill them. Prepare for the Cambrian Explosion by building species with high forage or attack values before it hits. Watch the 1st Ocean zone's population level to estimate when it will trigger.

walkthrough.title: Forage vs. Attack: Choosing Your Strategy
walkthrough.text: Foraging takes population from the Reef — a shared, depletable resource. Attacking takes population from other species — which can be unlimited if targets keep feeding. Foraging is simpler and more reliable in the early game. Attacking is riskier but can be devastating, especially if you target species loaded with population. A balanced approach often works best: one foraging species for steady income and one attacking species for big swings. Remember that you can attack your own species, which is useful for moving population around.

walkthrough.title: Evaluating Trait Cards
walkthrough.text: When looking at a trait card, ask three questions. First: does it help my species eat? Forage icons, attack icons, and gains triggers directly increase population. These are high priority. Second: does it protect my species? Defensive traits and shell icons keep population safe from attackers. Third: does it have a useful Migrate number? Even if you do not want the trait, a card with a high Migrate number can move a lot of population around the board. The best traits combine feeding ability with defensive or adjacency powers.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Adjacency is one of Oceans' most unique mechanics. Many traits trigger when an adjacent species feeds, generating gains from the Ocean. This means your species can accumulate population passively without using their own feeding phase. Build species with gains triggers next to species that feed frequently. Remember that adjacency wraps around the table — your leftmost species is adjacent to the previous player's rightmost species. This means other players' feeding can trigger your gains too, which is incredibly powerful.

Ignoring the Reef = foragers starve when it empties
Stacking traits on species that cannot feed
Hoarding Deep cards without score pile to pay costs
Forgetting ALL species age every turn, not just the fed one

Start with 1-2 well-fed species before adding more
Only add a 3rd species when existing ones sustain themselves
More species = more drain; quality over quantity

summary.title: The Power of Adjacency
bullets: Adjacent species gains trigger passively when a neighbor feeds
Place gains-trigger species next to frequent feeders
Adjacency wraps around the table — opponents' feeding can trigger your gains
Passive gains from Ocean do not deplete the Reef

walkthrough.title: The Foraging Engine
walkthrough.text: The purest strategy in Oceans is the foraging engine — one or two species stacked with forage icons that vacuum population from the Reef every turn. With 2-3 forage traits on a single species, you can pull 4-6 population per feeding. After aging, you still retain a healthy buffer. The key is sustainability: keep the Reef stocked by migrating population from Ocean zones. A dedicated foraging engine is consistent and low-risk, but it depends on the Reef having population. Watch for other players foraging too — if everyone builds foragers, the Reef drains fast.

walkthrough.title: The Predator Engine
walkthrough.text: Attack-focused strategies are high-risk, high-reward. An attacker with 3 red icons takes 3 population from any target species in a single feeding. Target species loaded with population — your own or opponents'. The predator engine does not rely on the Reef at all, which means it keeps working even when the Reef is dry. The risk is that opponents play defensive traits or shell icons that reduce your attack yield. Counter this by targeting species without defenses, or by using traits that bypass defensive traits. Always have a backup plan if your primary target goes extinct.

walkthrough.title: The Passive Gains Engine
walkthrough.text: This is the most elegant strategy in Oceans. Build species with gains triggers next to species that feed frequently. Every time an adjacent species feeds, your species gains population from the Ocean — no action required on your part. The gains come from the 1st Ocean zone, then the 2nd, then the 3rd, which means they help drain the Ocean and accelerate the game. The best passive gains engines trigger off opponents' feeding too, since adjacency wraps around the table. A species with multiple gains triggers next to an active feeder can accumulate 3-5 population per round without ever being the species you choose to feed.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Game Clock
walkthrough.text: The game ends when all 3 Ocean zones empty. Population that moves through gains comes from the Ocean, not the Reef. This means passive gains engines drain the Ocean and accelerate the endgame. If you are ahead in points, accelerating the clock is to your advantage — build more gains triggers to pull population out of the Ocean faster. If you are behind, slow down the clock by migrating population back into Ocean zones and focusing on Reef-based foraging instead of gains. The player who best controls the game clock has a massive strategic advantage.

walkthrough.title: Cambrian Explosion Timing
walkthrough.text: The Cambrian Explosion is a pivotal moment. After it triggers, you play 2 cards per turn and age 2 per species. If you have species with forage values of 3 or higher, the doubled aging is no problem — you still accumulate faster than you drain. If your species are thin, the doubled aging will crush them. Advanced players try to trigger the Cambrian Explosion at the perfect moment: when their species are strong enough to benefit from 2 cards and 2 aging, but before opponents are ready. Use migration to empty the 1st Ocean zone when you are prepared, or to stall it by migrating population back in when you are not.

walkthrough.title: Deep Card Economy
walkthrough.text: Deep cards are incredibly powerful but come with a cost: you pay population from your score pile equal to the card's Migrate number to evolve a species with it. This means every Deep card is a calculated gamble — you are trading guaranteed points for a trait that might earn you more. The best Deep cards are ones whose power generates more population than their cost over the remaining rounds. If a Deep card costs 4 and the game has 5 rounds left, it needs to net you more than 4 population to justify the investment. Late-game Deep cards with high costs are rarely worth it unless they completely change the board state.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Card Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Scenario cards change the rules for everyone, but you can position yourself to benefit more than opponents. Blue Scenarios activate when their Ocean zone is empty — if the effect benefits your species, migrate population away from that zone to activate it sooner. Purple Scenarios trigger each time the zone empties, which can happen multiple times if population is migrated back. Use this to your advantage: migrate population onto a Purple Scenario's zone, then let the game drain it again to trigger the event repeatedly. Understanding and exploiting Scenario cards separates intermediate players from advanced ones.

walkthrough.title: Overpopulation as a Tool
walkthrough.text: Overpopulation is usually seen as a penalty, but advanced players sometimes use it deliberately. When a species overpopulates, it drops to 5 population and the excess goes to the Reef or an Ocean zone. If your species has gains triggers that push it past 10 population, the overpopulation penalty actually converts excess population into board locations where it benefits you. Overpopulate into the Reef to keep your foragers fed. Overpopulate into an Ocean zone to stall the Cambrian Explosion. The fishbones space is a ceiling, not just a trap.

walkthrough.title: Species Positioning and Adjacency Chains
walkthrough.text: The order of your species matters more than most players realize. You choose where to place each new species: to the left, to the right, or between existing species. Build adjacency chains where one species feeding triggers gains on the next, which might trigger another effect. Your leftmost species is adjacent to the previous player's rightmost species, so pay attention to what your neighbors are building. If the player to your right has a heavy feeder, place a species with gains triggers on your left end to benefit from their activity.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, shift your focus from building to pure scoring. Every population behind your Player Screen is locked in as 1 point. Population on species boards also scores, but only if the species survives to game end. In the last round, consider letting weaker species go extinct if feeding a stronger species nets more total population. Use migration to empty Ocean zones faster if you are ahead, or to stall if you need more turns. Remember: surviving species score their remaining population, so a species with 5 population at game end is worth 5 extra points. Do not let species die unnecessarily in the final round.

bullets: Stack 2-3 forage traits on one species for 4-6 population per feeding
Use migration to keep the Reef stocked
Consistent and low-risk, but depends on Reef population
Watch for other foragers draining the shared Reef

summary.title: The Predator Engine
bullets: Attack icons let you take population directly from other species
Does not rely on the Reef — works even when Reef is empty
Target species loaded with population for maximum yield
Risk: defensive traits and shell icons reduce attack effectiveness

summary.title: The Passive Gains Engine
bullets: Gains triggers fire when adjacent species feeds — no action needed
Population comes from Ocean zones, not the Reef
Adjacency wraps around table — benefit from opponents' feeding
Can accumulate 3-5 population per round passively

summary.title: Controlling the Game Clock
bullets: Game ends when all 3 Ocean zones empty
Gains drain Ocean = accelerates endgame (good when ahead)
Migration back to Ocean = slows endgame (good when behind)
Clock control is a major strategic lever

Strong species benefit hugely; weak species get crushed
Migrate to trigger it early when ready, or stall when not

entries.definition: The primary resource and scoring unit in Oceans. The game contains 200 population tokens. Regardless of color, each token is worth 1 point at end of game if it is in your score pile or on a surviving species. Population flows from the board to species boards to score piles.

A board representing one of your species, with 10 spaces for population tokens. The 10th space has a fishbones icon — placing a token there triggers overpopulation. The game includes 24 species boards shared among all players.

A board location that holds population tokens. Species forage from the Reef using green forage icons. The Reef starts with population but does not naturally refill when emptied — population must be added through migration, overpopulation, or card effects.

A board with 3 zones that hold population tokens. The Ocean is considered limitless — when the 1st zone empties, population is taken from the 2nd, then the 3rd. Species gain population from the Ocean through blue gains icons. When the final zone empties, the last round triggers.
The Ocean board has 3 zones stacked vertically. Each zone holds population tokens and has a shelf for Scenario cards or the Cambrian Explosion card. When a zone empties, its Scenario card activates.
A deck of 120 cards containing 12 different traits with 10 copies of each. Surface cards represent the known world of marine biology. They are used to evolve species or migrate population.

A deck of 89 unique, powerful cards representing undiscovered marine life. Deep cards can only be played after the Cambrian Explosion starts. They cost population from your score pile to evolve a species, but can be used for migration at no cost.

Two face-up piles of Deep cards placed next to the Deep deck during setup. During Phase 4, players may take the top card from a Gene Pool pile, or draw 3 from the Deep deck, keep 1, and place the other 2 on the Gene Pool piles.

25 cards that modify the game rules. Two are placed on the 1st and 2nd Ocean zone shelves during setup. Blue Scenarios have ongoing effects when their zone is empty. Purple Scenarios trigger events each time their zone empties.

A card placed in the 1st Ocean zone during setup. When the 1st Ocean zone empties, the Cambrian Explosion begins permanently. The rate of evolution doubles: play 2 cards per turn and age 2 population per species. Has a Reef Variant side for beginner games.

One of 4 screens used to hide each player's score pile. Population scored during aging goes behind the screen. Score pile contents are revealed only at end of game. The game includes 4 Player Screens.

One of 4 tokens given during setup to offset the disadvantage of going early in turn order. Point values vary by player count: in a 4-player game, values are 7, 6, 4, and 0 for 1st through 4th player respectively.

60 population tokens set aside during setup in a zip-lock bag. These enter the game in two situations: when the 3rd Ocean zone empties during play (placed back in the 3rd zone so gains resolve properly), and when the final round is triggered (placed in the 3rd Ocean zone).

A number on the bottom left of every card (Surface and Deep). When playing a card to migrate instead of evolve, move this many population tokens from one board location to another. Also determines the cost of playing a Deep card as a trait.

A feeding action where a species takes population from the Reef. The forage value equals the total green forage icons on the species' traits. A species with no green icons has a default forage value of 1. A species with an attack-only restriction cannot forage.

A green icon on trait cards that adds to a species' forage value. Multiple forage icons on the same species are cumulative. Each green icon lets the species take 1 additional population from the Reef when foraging.

A feeding action where a species takes population from another species (including your own). The attack value equals the total red attack icons on the attacking species' traits. Default attack value is 1 if there are no red icons. A species with a forage-only restriction cannot attack.

A red icon on trait cards that adds to a species' attack value. Multiple attack icons on the same species are cumulative. Each red icon lets the species take 1 additional population from the target when attacking.

A passive way for species to acquire population from the Ocean. Gains icons (blue) are triggered by feeding events, often on adjacent species. Population is taken from the 1st Ocean zone, then 2nd, then 3rd. Gains can be triggered by any player's feeding.

A blue icon on trait cards indicating passive population acquisition from the Ocean. When triggered, the species takes the number shown in the icon from the 1st Ocean zone (then 2nd, then 3rd if earlier zones are empty).

Occurs when a species has a population token on the fishbones space (the 10th space on the species board). The species loses population until only 5 remain. Lost population goes to the Reef or any one Ocean zone (owner's choice). Self-overpopulation ends that species' feeding for the turn.

The 10th and final space on each species board, marked with a fishbones icon. Placing a population token here triggers overpopulation. You may only place a token here if it is the last available space.

A species goes extinct when it cannot age the full amount during Phase 3 (Aging). All traits are discarded and the species board returns to the communal supply. A species at 0 population on another player's turn does NOT go extinct — it survives until its owner's aging phase.

Phase 3 of each turn. Remove 1 population from each of your species (2 after Cambrian Explosion) and place them behind your Player Screen as points. All of your species age, not just the one that fed. Species that cannot age the full amount go extinct.

Population tokens placed behind your Player Screen during aging. Each token is worth 1 point at game end. Score pile tokens can be spent to pay the cost of Deep cards. Your score pile is hidden from other players until final scoring.

A trait with a black textbox that may prevent or reduce the effectiveness of attacks against a species. Some defensive traits completely block attacks; others have shell icons that reduce population taken.

An icon on defensive traits with a number inside. Each shell icon reduces the number of population taken from an attack by the shown number. Shell icons do not reduce the attacker's attack value — just the population transferred. Multiple shell icons stack.

Species on either side of another species are considered adjacent. Adjacency wraps around the table — your leftmost species is adjacent to the previous player's rightmost species. Many traits trigger based on adjacent species' feeding activity.

An icon at the bottom of some traits indicating which adjacent species the trait interacts with. The arrow points toward the species immediately adjacent on that side. Used for gains triggers, defensive coverage, and other adjacency effects.

A card played on a species board to give it abilities. Surface traits come from the Surface deck; Deep traits come from the Deep deck. Maximum 3 traits per species unless a card allows more. Traits can be removed anytime during Phase 1.

Playing a card as a trait on a species (new or existing). This is the primary use of cards during Phase 1. Most Surface cards are played to evolve. Deep cards have an additional cost to evolve (pay Migrate number from score pile).

An alternative use for a card during Phase 1. Instead of evolving, discard the card and move population tokens equal to its Migrate number from one board location to another. Deep cards used to migrate have no additional cost and are removed from the game.

A type of Scenario card with an ongoing effect. The effect is active whenever there is no population in the Scenario's Ocean zone. The effect deactivates if population is migrated back into the zone and reactivates when the zone empties again.

A type of Scenario card with an event that triggers each time its Ocean zone is emptied. This can happen multiple times if population is moved back onto the zone and it empties again. Events resolve after all feeding triggers (gains, overpopulation) are resolved.
A Scenario card used in the Reef Variant. Provides a beneficial effect that helps populate the ecosystem. Placed on the 1st Ocean zone shelf for beginner games instead of a random Scenario.
A Scenario card used in the Reef Variant. Provides a beneficial growth effect. Placed on the 2nd Ocean zone shelf for beginner games instead of a random Scenario.
An icon on Scenario cards indicating the card creates a more aggressive environment. Players who prefer less confrontation may remove these Scenarios during setup.
An icon on Scenario cards indicating the card is more complex. Players new to the game may want to remove these Scenarios during setup.
An icon on Scenario cards indicating the card triggers events (Purple Scenario type). Events happen each time the associated Ocean zone empties.

A simplified version of Oceans recommended for new players. Uses the Reef Variant side of the Cambrian Explosion card, the Abundance and Fertile Scenario cards, and does not use the Deep deck or Gene Pool. All other rules remain the same.

A feature allowing players to customize the Deep deck between games. After each game, remove Deep cards you did not enjoy and add copies of favorites. Over time, this creates a personalized version of Oceans tailored to your group's playstyle.

The first phase of each turn. Play 1 card (Surface only) before the Cambrian Explosion, or 2 cards (Surface and/or Deep) after. Cards are played to evolve species with traits or to migrate population. You may also freely remove traits from your species during this phase.

The second phase of each turn. Choose 1 of your species to feed. It may forage from the Reef (using green icons) or attack another species (using red icons). After feeding, resolve all triggered effects (gains, adjacency powers) and check for overpopulation.

The third phase of each turn. All of your species age. Remove 1 population per species (2 after Cambrian Explosion) and place behind Player Screen. Species that cannot age the full amount go extinct. This is the primary scoring mechanism.

The fourth and final phase of each turn. Optionally draw 1 Deep card (from Gene Pool or draw-3-keep-1). Optionally discard Surface cards. Draw from Surface deck until you hold 6 cards total. Play passes clockwise.

Triggered when all 3 Ocean zones are completely empty of population. The 60 reserve population tokens are placed in the 3rd Ocean zone, and play continues until the final player in turn order completes their turn.

At game end, each player scores: 1 point per population in their score pile, 1 point per population on surviving species, plus their Bonus Token value. Highest total wins. Tiebreaker: most trait cards on surviving species.

Face-up pile where discarded Surface cards are placed. Formed during setup by revealing the top Surface card. When the Surface deck runs out, the discard pile is shuffled to form a new deck. Deep cards are never discarded — they are removed from the game.

A Surface trait that allows a species to feed multiple times during Phase 2. One of the 12 Surface traits with 10 copies in the deck. If a species overpopulates during its own feeding with Tentacled, it cannot feed again that turn.
A Surface trait that interacts with feeding through a defensive or adjacency mechanism. One of the 12 Surface traits with 10 copies in the deck.
A Surface trait that triggers gains when an adjacent species feeds. Provides passive population acquisition from the Ocean. One of the 12 Surface traits with 10 copies in the deck.

A Surface trait that triggers gains from the Ocean when specific feeding conditions are met. Can provide substantial passive population (the example in the rulebook shows 2 gains per Bottom Feeder). One of the 12 Surface traits with 10 copies in the deck.

The first player in turn order. Determined during setup by who can hold their breath the longest (per the rulebook). The start player receives the highest Bonus Token value for their player count. Play proceeds clockwise from the start player.

The order in which rules are overridden: Scenario cards override core rules and apply to all species; Trait cards override core rules AND Scenario cards but only apply to the species they are on. Most traits have an understood 'This species' at the start of their text.
